"Washington, D.C."), [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia "Virginia"), and [Maryland](/wiki/Maryland "Maryland") produce East Coast hip-hop.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

**East Coast hip-hop** is a regional subgenre of [hip-hop music](/wiki/Hip-hop "Hip-hop") that originated in New York City during the 1970s.[[3]](#cite_note-Adaso-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) Hip-hop is recognized to have originated and evolved first in the [Bronx](/wiki/The_Bronx "The Bronx") borough of [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City").[[5]](#cite_note-Allmusic-5) In contrast to other styles, East Coast hip-hop music prioritizes complex lyrics for attentive listening rather than beats for dancing.[[5]](#cite_note-Allmusic-5) The term "East Coast hip-hop" more specifically denotes hip-hop originating from the Northeastern United States. [Southeastern](/wiki/Southeastern_United_States "Southeastern United States") states such as [Georgia](/wiki/Georgia_(U.S._state) "Georgia (U.S. state)") or Florida instead produce [Southern hip-hop](/wiki/Southern_hip-hop "Southern hip-hop") rather than East Coast hip-hop, although [the District of Columbia](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia "Virginia"), and [Maryland](/wiki/Maryland "Maryland") produce East Coast hip-hop.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## Musical style

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_Coast_hip-hop&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Musical style")]

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Gnome-mime-sound-openclipart.svg/60px-Gnome-mime-sound-openclipart.svg.png)

[Example](/wiki/File:The_Choice_Is_Yours_(Revisited).ogg "File:The Choice Is Yours (Revisited).ogg")

A sample of an East Coast hip-hop song, highlighting an emphasis on lyricism, as well as displaying boom bap and jazz rap elements. The song played on the background is "[The Choice Is Yours (Revisited)](/wiki/The_Choice_Is_Yours_(Revisited) "The Choice Is Yours (Revisited)")" by [Black Sheep](/wiki/Black_Sheep_(duo) "Black Sheep (duo)").

---

*Problems playing this file? See [media help](/wiki/Help:Media "Help:Media").*

In contrast to the more simplistic rhyme pattern and scheme used in [Old-school hip-hop](/wiki/Old-school_hip-hop "Old-school hip-hop"), hip-hop in the late 1980s developed a stronger emphasis on lyrical dexterity.[[3]](#cite_note-Adaso-3) It also became characterized by multi-syllabic rhymes, complex wordplay, a continuous free-flowing delivery and intricate metaphors.[[3]](#cite_note-Adaso-3) Although East Coast hip-hop can vary in sound and style, "aggressive" beats and the combining of [samples](/wiki/Sampling_(music) "Sampling (music)") were common to the subgenre in the mid- to late 1980s.[[5]](#cite_note-Allmusic-5)

The aggressive and hard-hitting beats of the form were emphasized by such acts as [EPMD](/wiki/EPMD "EPMD"), [Beastie Boys](/wiki/Beastie_Boys "Beastie Boys") and [Public Enemy](/wiki/Public_Enemy "Public Enemy"), while artists such as [Rakim](/wiki/Rakim "Rakim"), [Boogie Down Productions](/wiki/Boogie_Down_Productions "Boogie Down Productions"), [LL Cool J](/wiki/LL_Cool_J "LL Cool J"), [Big Daddy Kane](/wiki/Big_Daddy_Kane "Big Daddy Kane"), [Nas](/wiki/Nas "Nas"), [The Notorious B.I.G.](/wiki/The_Notorious_B.I.G. "The Notorious B.I.G."), and [Slick Rick](/wiki/Slick_Rick "Slick Rick") were noted for their lyrical skill. Lyrical themes throughout the history of East Coast hip-hop have ranged from [lyrical consciousness](/wiki/Political_hip_hop#Conscious_hip_hop "Political hip hop") by such artists as Public Enemy and [A Tribe Called Quest](/wiki/A_Tribe_Called_Quest "A Tribe Called Quest") to [Mafioso rap](/wiki/Mafioso_rap "Mafioso rap") themes by rappers such as [Raekwon](/wiki/Raekwon "Raekwon"), [MF Grimm](/wiki/MF_Grimm "MF Grimm") and [Kool G Rap](/wiki/Kool_G_Rap "Kool G Rap").[[3]](#cite_note-Adaso-3)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_Coast_hip-hop&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

### 1973–1986: Emergence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_Coast_hip-hop&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1973–1986: Emergence")]

Main articles: [Roots of hip hop](/wiki/Roots_of_hip_hop "Roots of hip hop"), [Old-school hip-hop](/wiki/Old-school_hip-hop "Old-school hip-hop"), and [New-school hip-hop](/wiki/New-school_hip-hop "New-school hip-hop")

See also: [The Bridge Wars](/wiki/The_Bridge_Wars "The Bridge Wars")

East Coast hip-hop is occasionally referred to as New York rap due to its origins and development at block parties thrown in New York City during the 1970s.[[3]](#cite_note-Adaso-3) According to [AllMusic](/wiki/AllMusic "AllMusic"), "At the dawn of the hip-hop era, all rap was East Coast rap."[[5]](#cite_note-Allmusic-5) Leading up to hip-hop, there were spoken-word artists such as [the Last Poets](/wiki/The_Last_Poets "The Last Poets") who released [their debut album](/wiki/The_Last_Poets_(album) "The Last Poets (album)") in 1970, and [Gil Scott-Heron](/wiki/Gil_Scott-Heron "Gil Scott-Heron"), who gained a wide audience with his 1971 track "[The Revolution Will Not Be Televised](/wiki/The_Revolution_Will_Not_Be_Televised "The Revolution Will Not Be Televised")". These artists combined spoken word and music to create a kind of "proto-rap" vibe.[[9]](#cite_note-9) Following this, early artists of hip-hop such as [DJ Kool Herc](/wiki/DJ_Kool_Herc "DJ Kool Herc"), [Grandmaster Flash](/wiki/Grandmaster_Flash "Grandmaster Flash"), [Afrika Bambaataa](/wiki/Afrika_Bambaataa "Afrika Bambaataa"), [the Sugarhill Gang](/wiki/The_Sugarhill_Gang "The Sugarhill Gang"), [Kurtis Blow](/wiki/Kurtis_Blow "Kurtis Blow"), [Jam Master Jay](/wiki/Jam_Master_Jay "Jam Master Jay") and [Run-DMC](/wiki/Run-DMC "Run-DMC"), pioneered East Coast hip-hop during hip-hop's earlier years in the 1970s and 1980s.[[5]](#cite_note-Allmusic-5)

### 1986–1997: Renaissance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_Coast_hip-hop&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1986–1997: Renaissance")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/RZA_7_Shankbone_2009_Tao_of_Wu.jpg/250px-RZA_7_Shankbone_2009_Tao_of_Wu.jpg)](/wiki/File:RZA_7_Shankbone_2009_Tao_of_Wu.jpg)

[RZA](/wiki/RZA "RZA"), producer and member of the [Wu-Tang Clan](/wiki/Wu-Tang_Clan "Wu-Tang Clan")

As the genre developed, lyrical themes evolved through the work of East Coast artists such as the [Native Tongues](/wiki/Native_Tongues "Native Tongues"), a collective of hip-hop artists associated with generally positive, Afrocentric themes, and assembled by Afrika Bambaataa. New York–based groups such as [De La Soul](/wiki/De_La_Soul "De La Soul"), [Public Enemy](/wiki/Public_Enemy "Public Enemy"), [A Tribe Called Quest](/wiki/A_Tribe_Called_Quest "A Tribe Called Quest"), and the [Jungle Brothers](/wiki/Jungle_Brothers "Jungle Brothers") also earned recognition for their musical eclecticism.[[5]](#cite_note-Allmusic-5) This period from the mid-1980s to mid-1990s has been called the "[golden age](/wiki/Golden_age_hip-hop "Golden age hip-hop")" of hip-hop. Although East Coast hip-hop was more popular throughout the late 1980s, [N.W.A](/wiki/N.W.A "N.W.A")'s *[Straight Outta Compton](/wiki/Straight_Outta_Compton "Straight Outta Compton")* (released in early 1989) presented the toughened sound of [West Coast hip-hop](/wiki/West_Coast_hip-hop "West Coast hip-hop"), which was accompanied by gritty, street-level subject matter.[[5]](#cite_note-Allmusic-5) Later in 1992, [Dr. Dre](/wiki/Dr._Dre "Dr. Dre")'s [G-funk](/wiki/G-funk "G-funk") record *[The Chronic](/wiki/The_Chronic "The Chronic")* would introduce West Coast hip-hop to the mainstream. Along with a combined ability to keep its primary function as party music, the West Coast form of hip-hop became a dominant force during the early 1990s.[[5]](#cite_note-Allmusic-5) Although G-Funk was the most popular variety of hip-hop during the early 1990s, the East Coast hip-hop scene remained an integral part of the music industry. During this period, several New York City rappers rising from the local underground scene, began releasing noteworthy albums in the early and mid-1990s, such as [Nas](/wiki/Nas "Nas"), [The Notorious B.I.G.](/wiki/The_Notorious_B.I.G. "The Notorious B.I.G.") and others.[[10]](#cite_note-Stylus-10) [The Stretch Armstrong and Bobbito Show](/wiki/The_Stretch_Armstrong_and_Bobbito_Show "The Stretch Armstrong and Bobbito Show") was the launch pad for many East Coast rappers during this era.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Nas-04.jpg/250px-Nas-04.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nas-04.jpg)

[Nas](/wiki/Nas "Nas")'s 1994 debut album *[Illmatic](/wiki/Illmatic "Illmatic")* was critically acclaimed.

[Nas](/wiki/Nas "Nas")' 1994 debut album *[Illmatic](/wiki/Illmatic "Illmatic")* has also been noted as one of the creative high points of the East Coast hip-hop scene, and featured production from such renowned New York–based producers as [Large Professor](/wiki/Large_Professor "Large Professor"), [Pete Rock](/wiki/Pete_Rock "Pete Rock") and [DJ Premier](/wiki/DJ_Premier "DJ Premier").[[10]](#cite_note-Stylus-10) Meanwhile, The [Wu-Tang Clan](/wiki/Wu-Tang_Clan "Wu-Tang Clan"), [Onyx](/wiki/Onyx_(hip-hop_group) "Onyx (hip-hop group)"), [Black Moon](/wiki/Black_Moon_(group) "Black Moon (group)"), [Smif-N-Wessun](/wiki/Smif-N-Wessun "Smif-N-Wessun"), [Big L](/wiki/Big_L "Big L"), [Lost Boyz](/wiki/Lost_Boyz "Lost Boyz") and [Mobb Deep](/wiki/Mobb_Deep "Mobb Deep") became pillars in New York's [hardcore hip-hop](/wiki/Hardcore_hip-hop "Hardcore hip-hop") scene, achieving widespread critical acclaim for their landmark albums, *[Enter the Wu-Tang (36 Chambers)](/wiki/Enter_the_Wu-Tang_(36_Chambers) "Enter the Wu-Tang (36 Chambers)")* (1993), *[Lifestylez ov da Poor & Dangerous](/wiki/Lifestylez_ov_da_Poor_%26_Dangerous "Lifestylez ov da Poor & Dangerous")* (1995), *[Enta da Stage](/wiki/Enta_da_Stage "Enta da Stage")* (1993), *[Bacdafucup](/wiki/Bacdafucup "Bacdafucup")* (1993), *[Dah Shinin'](/wiki/Dah_Shinin%27 "Dah Shinin'")* (1995), *[Legal Drug Money](/wiki/Legal_Drug_Money "Legal Drug Money")* (1996) and *[The Infamous](/wiki/The_Infamous "The Infamous")* (1995).

[The Notorious B.I.G.](/wiki/The_Notorious_B.I.G. "The Notorious B.I.G.") became the central figure in East Coast hip-hop during most of the 1990s. [Bad Boy Records](/wiki/Bad_Boy_Records "Bad Boy Records") comprised a team of producers known as the Hitmen Stevie J, Derrick "D Dot" Angelletie and Amen Ra directed by [Sean Combs](/wiki/Sean_Combs "Sean Combs") to move the focus on hip-hop to New York with the Notorious B.I.G.'s Billboard topping hits.[[11]](#cite_note-BIGbio-11) His success on the music charts and rise to the mainstream drew more attention to New York at the time of West Coast hip-hop's dominance.[[11]](#cite_note-BIGbio-11) According to [AllMusic](/wiki/AllMusic "AllMusic") editor Steve Huey, the success of his 1994 debut album *[Ready to Die](/wiki/Ready_to_Die "Ready to Die")* "reinvented East Coast rap for the gangsta age" and "turned the Notorious B.I.G. into a hip-hop sensation — the first major star the East Coast had produced since the rise of Dr. Dre's West Coast G-funk".[[11]](#cite_note-BIGbio-11) Many saw his dominating presence as a catalyzing factor in the East Coast/West Coast hip-hop rivalry that polarized much of the hip-hop community, stirring the issue enough to result in the Brooklyn rapper's 1997 death, as well as his West Coast counterpart, [Tupac Shakur](/wiki/Tupac_Shakur "Tupac Shakur"), months prior.[[12]](#cite_note-12) By the late 1990s, East Coast rap had returned to mainstream dominance.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

### 1997–2007: Bling era, mainstream success

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_Coast_hip-hop&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1997–2007: Bling era, mainstream success")]

Biggie's commercial success helped pave the way for the success of other up-and-coming East Coast rappers such as [Jay-Z](/wiki/Jay-Z "Jay-Z"), [DMX](/wiki/DMX "DMX"), [Busta Rhymes](/wiki/Busta_Rhymes "Busta Rhymes"), [50 Cent](/wiki/50_Cent "50 Cent"), [Ja Rule](/wiki/Ja_Rule "Ja Rule"), [the Lox](/wiki/The_Lox "The Lox"), [Fat Joe](/wiki/Fat_Joe "Fat Joe"), [Big Pun](/wiki/Big_Pun "Big Pun"), and [Clipse](/wiki/Clipse "Clipse").[[11]](#cite_note-BIGbio-11)[[14]](#cite_note-14) Many East Coast hip-hop producers also rose to prominence during this period such as [Timbaland](/wiki/Timbaland "Timbaland"), [Pharrell](/wiki/Pharrell_Williams "Pharrell Williams"), [Just Blaze](/wiki/Just_Blaze "Just Blaze"), [Swizz Beatz](/wiki/Swizz_Beatz "Swizz Beatz"), [Irv Gotti](/wiki/Irv_Gotti "Irv Gotti"), and [7 Aurelius](/wiki/Channel_7_(musician) "Channel 7 (musician)")[[i]](#cite_note-15).

### 2007–2013: Blog era and revitalization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_Coast_hip-hop&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2007–2013: Blog era and revitalization")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Wiz_Khalifa_in_Under_The_Influence_Tour.jpg/250px-Wiz_Khalifa_in_Under_The_Influence_Tour.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wiz_Khalifa_in_Under_The_Influence_Tour.jpg)

Wiz Khalifa performing in Toronto in 2012.

A mainstream revitalization of East Coast rap occurred in the late 2000s and early 2010s, albeit without the same level of ubiquity as in the 1990s. Younger artists at this time used Internet resources such as social media, blogging, and [music streaming](/wiki/Music_streaming "Music streaming") to build a following among fans,[[15]](#cite_note-16)[[16]](#cite_note-17)[[17]](#cite_note-18) blurring the lines between the [underground](/wiki/Underground_hip-hop "Underground hip-hop") and the mainstream. Rappers who emerged during this "blog era" include [Joey Bada$$](/wiki/Joey_Badass "Joey Badass"), [A$AP Rocky](/wiki/ASAP_Rocky "ASAP Rocky"), [Nicki Minaj](/wiki/Nicki_Minaj "Nicki Minaj"), [Wiz Khalifa](/wiki/Wiz_Khalifa "Wiz Khalifa"), [Meek Mill](/wiki/Meek_Mill "Meek Mill"), [French Montana](/wiki/French_Montana "French Montana"), [Pusha T](/wiki/Pusha_T "Pusha T"), [Logic](/wiki/Logic_(rapper) "Logic (rapper)"), [Mac Miller](/wiki/Mac_Miller "Mac Miller"), [Vast Aire](/wiki/Vast_Aire "Vast Aire"), [Wale](/wiki/Wale_(rapper) "Wale (rapper)"), [Azealia Banks](/wiki/Azealia_Banks "Azealia Banks"), [Flatbush Zombies](/wiki/Flatbush_Zombies "Flatbush Zombies"), [Troy Ave](/wiki/Troy_Ave "Troy Ave"), [Bishop Nehru](/wiki/Bishop_Nehru "Bishop Nehru"), [Asher Roth](/wiki/Asher_Roth "Asher Roth"), and [Ka](/wiki/Ka_(rapper) "Ka (rapper)").

### 2014–present: Rise of trap, drill, boom-bap resurgence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_Coast_hip-hop&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2014–present: Rise of trap, drill, boom-bap resurgence")]

East Coast rappers that saw success during this period include [Cardi B](/wiki/Cardi_B "Cardi B"), [Lil Uzi Vert](/wiki/Lil_Uzi_Vert "Lil Uzi Vert"), [Fetty Wap](/wiki/Fetty_Wap "Fetty Wap"), [A Boogie wit da Hoodie](/wiki/A_Boogie_wit_da_Hoodie "A Boogie wit da Hoodie"), [Rich The Kid](/wiki/Rich_The_Kid "Rich The Kid"), [Tekashi 6ix9ine](/wiki/Tekashi_6ix9ine "Tekashi 6ix9ine"), [Sheck Wes](/wiki/Sheck_Wes "Sheck Wes"), [ASAP Ferg](/wiki/ASAP_Ferg "ASAP Ferg"), [Rico Nasty](/wiki/Rico_Nasty "Rico Nasty"), [Young M.A](/wiki/Young_M.A "Young M.A"), [Action Bronson](/wiki/Action_Bronson "Action Bronson"), [Shy Glizzy](/wiki/Shy_Glizzy "Shy Glizzy"), [YBN Cordae](/wiki/YBN_Cordae "YBN Cordae"), [Brent Faiyaz](/wiki/Brent_Faiyaz "Brent Faiyaz"), [GoldLink](/wiki/GoldLink "GoldLink"), [PnB Rock](/wiki/PnB_Rock "PnB Rock"), [Flipp Dinero](/wiki/Flipp_Dinero "Flipp Dinero"), and [Jay Critch](/wiki/Jay_Critch "Jay Critch"). Many of the rappers of this era gained prominence on [social media](/wiki/Social_media "Social media"), and some diverged from the traditional East Coast sound with stylistic choices that befitted the [streaming era](/wiki/Music_streaming_service "Music streaming service"), such as [trap](/wiki/Trap_music "Trap music") production and [Southern](/wiki/Southern_hip-hop "Southern hip-hop") hip-hop influence.[[18]](#cite_note-19) Various factors led to a decline in unique regional scenes, including East Coast rap, and rivalries between different cities and regions declined significantly, with artists across different regions and genres more willing to collaborate than in the past.[[19]](#cite_note-20)

[New York City's drill](/wiki/Brooklyn_drill "Brooklyn drill") genre, heavily influenced by [UK drill](/wiki/UK_drill "UK drill") (and often using the same London producers, such as [808Melo](/wiki/808Melo "808Melo")), has injected new energy into the New York hip-hop scene, attracting critical acclaim, media controversy and a significant following, despite departing from standard hip-hop song structures.[[20]](#cite_note-NPRdrill-21) The genre started in Brooklyn, led by artists such as [Bobby Shmurda](/wiki/Bobby_Shmurda "Bobby Shmurda"), the late [Pop Smoke](/wiki/Pop_Smoke "Pop Smoke"), [Fivio Foreign](/wiki/Fivio_Foreign "Fivio Foreign"), [Sheff G](/wiki/Sheff_G "Sheff G"), and [22Gz](/wiki/22Gz "22Gz"). [Bronx drill](/wiki/Brooklyn_drill#Bronx_drill "Brooklyn drill"), a related subgenre, has also emerged, with prominent rappers including [Kay Flock](/wiki/Kay_Flock "Kay Flock"), [Ice Spice](/wiki/Ice_Spice "Ice Spice") and [Kenzo B](/wiki/Kenzo_B "Kenzo B").[[21]](#cite_note-22)

A neo-[mafioso](/wiki/Gangsta_rap#Mafioso_rap "Gangsta rap") style of East Coast rap, marked by an emphasis on "grimy" gangster lyricism, wordplay, and [boom-bap](/wiki/Boom_bap "Boom bap") production, reminiscent of "classic" mid-90's acts such as "Wu-Tang, Mobb Deep, Onyx, and early Jay-Z",[[22]](#cite_note-23) has made an emergence, garnering critical and commercial success, and a [cult following](/wiki/Cult_following "Cult following").[[23]](#cite_note-24)[[24]](#cite_note-25)[[25]](#cite_note-26) This modern revitalization of the traditional sound (sometimes referred to as "[coke](/wiki/Cocaine "Cocaine") rap"[[26]](#cite_note-27)[[27]](#cite_note-28)) has been spearheaded by [Griselda Records](/wiki/Griselda_Records "Griselda Records") of [Buffalo](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York"), [upstate](/wiki/Upstate_New_York "Upstate New York") New York, and its flagship artists [Westside Gunn](/wiki/Westside_Gunn "Westside Gunn"), [Benny the Butcher](/wiki/Benny_the_Butcher "Benny the Butcher"), and [Conway the Machine](/wiki/Conway_the_Machine "Conway the Machine"), as well as affiliated artists [Mach-Hommy](/wiki/Mach-Hommy "Mach-Hommy"), [Roc Marciano](/wiki/Roc_Marciano "Roc Marciano"), [Boldy James](/wiki/Boldy_James "Boldy James"), [Rome Streetz](/wiki/Rome_Streetz "Rome Streetz"), [Armani Caesar](/wiki/Armani_Caesar "Armani Caesar"), [Daringer](/wiki/Daringer_(producer) "Daringer (producer)") and [Conductor Williams](/wiki/Conductor_Williams "Conductor Williams"), among others.

## Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_Coast_hip-hop&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Legacy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/WEHO2022-6_%2852122368631%29.jpg/250px-WEHO2022-6_%2852122368631%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:WEHO2022-6_(52122368631).jpg)

Lil Kim's fourth studio album *[The Naked Truth](/wiki/The_Naked_Truth_(Lil%27_Kim_album) "The Naked Truth (Lil' Kim album)")* is the only album by a female rapper to have received five mics from *[The Source](/wiki/The_Source_(magazine) "The Source (magazine)")* for its outstanding lyrical performance. Lil Kim performs at a [pride parade](/wiki/Pride_parade "Pride parade") in Los Angeles in 2022 above.

East Coast hip-hop was the dominant form of rap music during the *[Golden Era of hip-hop](/wiki/Golden_age_hip-hop "Golden age hip-hop")*.[[3]](#cite_note-Adaso-3) Many knowledgeable hip-hop fans and critics are particularly favorable towards East Coast hip-hop of the early-mid 1990s, viewing it as a time of creative growth and influential recordings, and describing it as "The East Coast Renaissance". Music writer May Blaize of MVRemix Urban comments on the nostalgia felt among hip-hop fans for records released during this time:

> *It was claimed as the East Coast Renaissance. Wu-Tang brought the ruckus with* [36 Chambers](/wiki/Enter_the_Wu-Tang_(36_Chambers) "Enter the Wu-Tang (36 Chambers)")*. The world was ours when Nas released* [Illmatic](/wiki/Illmatic "Illmatic")*. [Big L](/wiki/Big_L "Big L"), (The MVP) came out with* [Lifestylez ov da Poor and Dangerous](/wiki/Lifestylez_ov_da_Poor_and_Dangerous "Lifestylez ov da Poor and Dangerous")*. Temperatures rose in clubs when Mobb Deep came out with* [The Infamous](/wiki/The_Infamous "The Infamous") *and Brooklyn's finest Jay-Z released* [Reasonable Doubt](/wiki/Reasonable_Doubt_(album) "Reasonable Doubt (album)")*. . . And who can forget the powerful uplifting anthem that would brand New York's concrete "Bucktown" ([Smif-n-Wessun](/wiki/Smif-n-Wessun "Smif-n-Wessun")'s hit single*)? . . .Ahh, it was a beautiful time in hip-hop history that many of us wish we could return to.[[28]](#cite_note-29)

David Drake of *[Stylus Magazine](/wiki/Stylus_Magazine "Stylus Magazine")* writes of hip-hop during 1994 and its contributions, stating: "The beats were hot, the rhymes were hot – it really was an amazing time for hip-hop and music in general. This was the critical point for the East Coast, a time when rappers from the New York area were releasing bucketloads of thrilling work – [Digable Planets](/wiki/Digable_Planets "Digable Planets"), [Gang Starr](/wiki/Gang_Starr "Gang Starr"), [Pete Rock](/wiki/Pete_Rock "Pete Rock"), [Jeru](/wiki/Jeru_tha_Damaja "Jeru tha Damaja"), [O.C.](/wiki/O.C._(rapper) "O.C. (rapper)"), [Organized Konfusion](/wiki/Organized_Konfusion "Organized Konfusion") – I mean, this was a year of serious music."[[10]](#cite_note-Stylus-10)

East Coast hip-hop has also produced a multitude of acclaimed female rappers, including [Salt](/wiki/Salt_(rapper) "Salt (rapper)") and [Pepa](/wiki/Pepa_(rapper) "Pepa (rapper)"), [The Real Roxanne](/wiki/The_Real_Roxanne "The Real Roxanne"), [Monie Love](/wiki/Monie_Love "Monie Love"), [Queen Latifah](/wiki/Queen_Latifah "Queen Latifah"), [Lil Kim](/wiki/Lil_Kim "Lil Kim"), [Lauryn Hill](/wiki/Lauryn_Hill "Lauryn Hill"), [Ladybug Mecca](/wiki/Digable_Planets "Digable Planets"), [Foxy Brown](/wiki/Foxy_Brown_(rapper) "Foxy Brown (rapper)"), [Charli Baltimore](/wiki/Charli_Baltimore "Charli Baltimore"), [Eve](/wiki/Eve_(rapper) "Eve (rapper)"), [Missy Elliott](/wiki/Missy_Elliott "Missy Elliott"), [Angie Martinez](/wiki/Angie_Martinez "Angie Martinez"), [Remy Ma](/wiki/Remy_Ma "Remy Ma"), [Lil Mama](/wiki/Lil_Mama "Lil Mama"), [Nicki Minaj](/wiki/Nicki_Minaj "Nicki Minaj"), [Cardi B](/wiki/Cardi_B "Cardi B"), [Rapsody](/wiki/Rapsody "Rapsody"), [Young M.A](/wiki/Young_M.A "Young M.A"), [BIA](/wiki/Bia_(rapper) "Bia (rapper)"), [Armani Caesar](/wiki/Armani_Caesar "Armani Caesar"), [Ice Spice](/wiki/Ice_Spice "Ice Spice"), and [Coi Leray](/wiki/Coi_Leray "Coi Leray").
